description: The same Mandelbrot.java as java-javac-openjdk, byte for byte, compiled by the same javac and run on the same HotSpot -- with Graal replacing C2 as the JIT. Same bytecode, same VM, same GC: the code generator is the only variable.
comments: Floating point is strict here as everywhere on the JVM: the JLS binds the JIT as tightly as it binds javac, so Graal may no more contract a multiply-add than C2 may. The entrypoint passes -XX:+EnableJVMCI -XX:+UseJVMCICompiler explicitly rather than trusting a default: a silent fall back to C2 would make this row java-javac-openjdk under another name, publishing a difference that is really just noise. HotSpot refuses to start on a flag it does not know, so a future GraalVM that drops JVMCI fails the campaign instead of quietly measuring the wrong compiler.

description: The same Mandelbrot.java as java-javac-openjdk, byte for byte, compiled by the same javac and executed by Eclipse OpenJ9 instead of HotSpot. A different JIT and a different GC underneath identical bytecode: whatever separates the two rows is the virtual machine.
comments: Floating point is strict here too, and by the same rule: the JLS binds any JVM that claims the name, so Testarossa may no more contract a multiply-add than C2 may. One quirk is worth carrying, because it makes every future -XX: flag on this row suspect — OpenJ9 silently ignores options it does not know: -XX:CompleteNonsenseFlag=42 starts it happily, where HotSpot refuses to boot. A flag passed here can therefore pin nothing while looking as though it pinned something, and this entrypoint passes none: neither -Xquickstart nor -Xshareclasses, no vector cap, nothing. This is the VM as it ships.

description: The reference kernel in Java, compiled by javac and run on HotSpot with platform threads and an AtomicInteger work queue. No Maven, no Gradle, no jar: one file, one javac invocation, so the Build column times the compiler and nothing else.
comments: Floating point is strict here and could not be anything else: since JEP 306 the JLS forbids javac — and every JIT — from contracting a multiply-add, so fusing is Math.fma and the source has to write it. The JVM is handed nothing but the classpath: no -Xss, no -Xmx, no -XX:TieredStopAtLevel, no cap on vector width. The defaults are what a Java program gets. javac is what the Build column times; C2 compiles during the run, and its time is billed there.

description: The same Mandelbrot.java as java-javac-openjdk, byte for byte, compiled ahead of time by GraalVM native-image into a standalone ELF. No JVM at run time, no JIT, no class loading -- the program starts like a C binary because it is one.
comments: javac still runs, as a step in this compiler's pipeline rather than as the compiler: what ships is an ELF with the garbage collector and the runtime linked into it, and no JVM is installed in the image. The only JVM row with a true -march — though on AArch64 native-image offers no armv8.2-a and takes armv8.1-a: one level BELOW the campaign's baseline, never above, and the sample's isa column says armv8.1-a where its mode says baseline. Floating point is strict in both modes and cannot be otherwise: the JLS binds Graal ahead of time exactly as it binds C2 on the fly, so a wider ISA changes which instructions are emitted and never what they compute.

description: The same Mandelbrot.java as java-javac-openjdk, byte for byte, compiled ahead of time by GraalVM native-image into a standalone ELF. No JVM at run time, no JIT, no class loading -- the program starts like a C binary because it is one.
comments: javac still runs, as a step in this compiler's pipeline rather than as the compiler: what ships is an ELF with the garbage collector and the runtime linked into it, and no JVM is installed in the image. The only JVM row with a true -march — though on AArch64 native-image offers no armv8.2-a and takes armv8.1-a: one level BELOW the campaign's baseline, never above, and the sample's isa column says armv8.1-a where its mode says baseline. Floating point is strict in both modes and cannot be otherwise: the JLS binds Graal ahead of time exactly as it binds C2 on the fly, so a wider ISA changes which instructions are emitted and never what they compute.

description: The same mandelbrot.mjs as js-nodejs and js-deno, byte for byte, executed by Bun on JavaScriptCore. Node and Deno share V8; this is the only JavaScript row with a different engine underneath, which makes it the only one where a gap is about the compiler rather than the runtime around it.
comments: The build phase is `bun build` — a transpile and a bundle. Node's is a parse and Deno's a type check: the three runtimes do not agree on what happens before a run, and each build number describes its own runtime's idea of it.

description: The same mandelbrot.mjs as js-nodejs, byte for byte, executed by Deno -- which runs the same V8. Any gap between these two rows is the runtime around the engine: module resolution, the permission checks, and how quickly it can boot a worker isolate.
comments: The build phase is `deno check`, a full type check, where js-nodejs runs `node --check`, a parse. DENO_DIR ships warm with the node typings, because a measured run has no network to fetch them from.

description: The kernel as written, executed by Node.js on V8. Parallelism comes from node:worker_threads -- one isolate per worker -- with the row queue in a SharedArrayBuffer and Atomics.add handing rows out, which is as close to the C kernel's atomic cursor as the language gets.
comments: The build phase is `node --check` — parse and compile, no execution. The same mandelbrot.mjs runs under js-deno and js-bun, byte for byte, so what those three rows vary is the runtime and not the program.

description: The reference kernel in Julia, JIT-compiled through LLVM on first call, with Threads.@spawn tasks pulling rows off an atomic cursor. The ISA target is passed explicitly to the JIT, in both modes -- Julia is one of the few runtimes that compiles at run time and still lets you say which machine to compile for.
comments: The JIT compiles on first call, inside the measured run: a script's native code is cached nowhere, so every run pays for it again. There is no warm-up phase to hide that in, and hiding it would be a different benchmark. `--cpu-target` is passed on every invocation, and the baseline mode is the reason it must be: Julia's own default is `native`, so a forgotten flag does not fail the build -- it compiles for the bench machine and files the result under `baseline`. Floating-point needs no flag in either mode: Julia fuses a multiply-add only where the source writes `muladd`, and never reassociates.

description: The reference kernel in Julia, JIT-compiled through LLVM on first call, with Threads.@spawn tasks pulling rows off an atomic cursor. The ISA target is passed explicitly to the JIT, in both modes -- Julia is one of the few runtimes that compiles at run time and still lets you say which machine to compile for.

There is no warm-up phase to hide that in, and hiding it would be a different benchmark. `--cpu-target` is passed on every invocation, and the baseline mode is the reason it must be: Julia's own default is `native`, so a forgotten flag does not fail the build -- it compiles for the bench machine and files the result under `baseline`. Floating-point needs no flag in either mode: Julia fuses a multiply-add only where the source writes `muladd`, and never reassociates.

description: The same Mandelbrot.kt as kotlin-kotlinc-openjdk, byte for byte, compiled by the same kotlinc -- with Graal replacing C2 as the JIT. One cell of the (language x JVM) grid.
comments: One cell of the (language × JVM) grid: same bytecode, same VM, same GC, a different code generator. The entrypoint passes -XX:+EnableJVMCI -XX:+UseJVMCICompiler explicitly rather than trusting a default: a silent fall back to C2 would make this row kotlin-kotlinc-openjdk under another name. Floating point is the JLS's, and Graal is bound by it exactly as C2 is.

description: The same Mandelbrot.kt as kotlin-kotlinc-openjdk, byte for byte, compiled by the same kotlinc and executed by Eclipse OpenJ9 instead of HotSpot.
comments: A different virtual machine, not a different build of HotSpot: OpenJ9 brings its own JIT (Testarossa), its own GC and its own object layout, under bytecode that is byte for byte the other Kotlin rows'. Floating point is still the JLS's, and still strict. One quirk is worth carrying, because it makes any future -XX: flag on this row suspect: OpenJ9 silently ignores options it does not know, where HotSpot refuses to boot on them. This entrypoint passes none.

description: The kernel in Kotlin, compiled by kotlinc to JVM bytecode and run on the same HotSpot as java-javac-openjdk. Same platform threads, same AtomicInteger queue, same arithmetic -- the language in front of the JIT is the only thing that changes.
comments: Floating point is the JVM's, inherited whole: a Double is an IEEE 754 double, and neither kotlinc nor the JIT may contract a multiply-add. One kotlinc invocation on one file — no Gradle, no Maven, no jar — so the build phase times the compiler and nothing else. The Kotlin standard library is on the classpath, and the JVM loads it at startup. The JVM is handed nothing else: no -Xmx, no cap on vector width, the defaults a Kotlin program gets.

description: The same Mandelbrot.kt as every other Kotlin row, compiled ahead of time by GraalVM native-image into a standalone ELF -- with the Kotlin standard library swallowed whole and linked in. kotlinc runs first, as a step in this compiler's pipeline, exactly as javac does for java-native-image.
comments: kotlinc runs first, as a step in this compiler's pipeline, exactly as javac does for java-native-image; native-image then has to analyse and link the Kotlin standard library into the ELF. On AArch64 it takes armv8.1-a: one level BELOW the campaign's baseline, never above, and the sample's isa column says so where its mode says baseline. Floating point is strict in both modes and cannot be otherwise: the JLS binds Graal ahead of time exactly as it binds C2 on the fly, so a wider ISA changes which instructions are emitted and never what they compute.

description: The same Mandelbrot.kt as every other Kotlin row, compiled ahead of time by GraalVM native-image into a standalone ELF -- with the Kotlin standard library swallowed whole and linked in. kotlinc runs first, as a step in this compiler's pipeline, exactly as javac does for java-native-image.
comments: kotlinc runs first, as a step in this compiler's pipeline, exactly as javac does for java-native-image; native-image then has to analyse and link the Kotlin standard library into the ELF. On AArch64 it takes armv8.1-a: one level BELOW the campaign's baseline, never above, and the sample's isa column says so where its mode says baseline. Floating point is strict in both modes and cannot be otherwise: the JLS binds Graal ahead of time exactly as it binds C2 on the fly, so a wider ISA changes which instructions are emitted and never what they compute.

description: The kernel as written, executed by the CPython interpreter. Parallelism comes from multiprocessing with the fork start method, because the GIL serialises CPU-bound bytecode -- threading here would measure the GIL, not the machine.
comments: Forking the pool is inside the timer on purpose: what a parallel runtime costs to start is part of what that runtime costs. The row reports `isa: distro`, not the campaign's baseline: no `-march` appears in this interpreter's own CFLAGS or configure line, so its eval loop was built for the toolchain's floor -- plain `x86-64` (v1) -- one level below the baseline every compiled row here was held to, and chosen by the packager rather than by this campaign. What that costs is bounded by what the code is: an eval loop is a branchy dispatch over one bytecode at a time, with nothing to vectorise, so the instructions v3 adds over v1 are mostly instructions it has no use for. It goes in a column because it is true, not because it is large.

description: The same mandelbrot.py as python-cpython, byte for byte, compiled by Cython to a C extension module instead of interpreted. Cython is the compiler here, exactly as gcc is for the C kernel -- not a library the kernel imports.
comments: The source is python-cpython's, byte for byte, and that includes the absence of type annotations — annotating it would be a different program, and this row is here to compile the same one. Unannotated, the generated C manipulates PyFloat objects through the C-API rather than raw doubles. gcc is handed `-ffp-contract=off` in every mode: the ISA target decides which instructions it may emit, never what the arithmetic means. The `isa` it reports is the `-march` it was given, verbatim — gcc is the vocabulary the harness already speaks.

description: The same mandelbrot.py as python-cpython, byte for byte, compiled by Cython to a C extension module instead of interpreted. Cython is the compiler here, exactly as gcc is for the C kernel -- not a library the kernel imports.
comments: The source is python-cpython's, byte for byte, and that includes the absence of type annotations — annotating it would be a different program, and this row is here to compile the same one. Unannotated, the generated C manipulates PyFloat objects through the C-API rather than raw doubles. gcc is handed `-ffp-contract=off` in every mode: the ISA target decides which instructions it may emit, never what the arithmetic means. The `isa` it reports is the `-march` it was given, verbatim — gcc is the vocabulary the harness already speaks.

description: The same mandelbrot.py as python-cpython, byte for byte, executed by PyPy. The source is unchanged, the multiprocessing pool is unchanged; the difference is a tracing JIT that turns the hot loop into machine code while it runs.
comments: The JIT compiles the hot loop inside the measured run, because that is where it happens: there is no warm-up phase to hide it in, and hiding it would be a different benchmark. The source and the multiprocessing pool are python-cpython's, unchanged.

description: The reference kernel in Rust, compiled by rustc at -O3 against the ISA the mode names, with std::thread scoped threads and an AtomicU32 work queue. No cargo, no rayon, no dependency: one file, one rustc invocation.
comments: rustc only *warns* when it does not recognise an ISA target, then hands back a generic binary — so the entrypoint translates the harness's gcc-spelled -march into LLVM's spelling, and reads rustc's stderr back to refuse a binary it ignored the flag for. It links std statically: the binary carries the Rust runtime rather than loading it.

description: The reference kernel in Rust, compiled by rustc at -O3 against the ISA the mode names, with std::thread scoped threads and an AtomicU32 work queue. No cargo, no rayon, no dependency: one file, one rustc invocation.
comments: rustc only *warns* when it does not recognise an ISA target, then hands back a generic binary — so the entrypoint translates the harness's gcc-spelled -march into LLVM's spelling, and reads rustc's stderr back to refuse a binary it ignored the flag for. It links std statically: the binary carries the Rust runtime rather than loading it.

description: The same Mandelbrot.scala as every other Scala row, compiled ahead of time by GraalVM native-image into a standalone ELF -- with the Scala runtime linked in. scalac runs first, as a step in this compiler's pipeline.
comments: scalac runs first, as a step in this compiler's pipeline; native-image then analyses and links the whole Scala runtime — the 3.x library plus the 2.13 one it still stands on — into the ELF. On AArch64 it takes armv8.1-a: one level BELOW the campaign's baseline, never above, and the sample's isa column says so where its mode says baseline. Floating point is strict in both modes and cannot be otherwise: the JLS binds Graal ahead of time exactly as it binds C2 on the fly. Not to be confused with scala-scala-native, which is Scala Native through LLVM: a different compiler entirely, and one that takes the -march verbatim.

description: The same Mandelbrot.scala as every other Scala row, compiled ahead of time by GraalVM native-image into a standalone ELF -- with the Scala runtime linked in. scalac runs first, as a step in this compiler's pipeline.
comments: scalac runs first, as a step in this compiler's pipeline; native-image then analyses and links the whole Scala runtime — the 3.x library plus the 2.13 one it still stands on — into the ELF. On AArch64 it takes armv8.1-a: one level BELOW the campaign's baseline, never above, and the sample's isa column says so where its mode says baseline. Floating point is strict in both modes and cannot be otherwise: the JLS binds Graal ahead of time exactly as it binds C2 on the fly. Not to be confused with scala-scala-native, which is Scala Native through LLVM: a different compiler entirely, and one that takes the -march verbatim.

description: The same Mandelbrot.scala as scala-scalac-openjdk, byte for byte, compiled by Scala Native through LLVM into a standalone ELF. No JVM, no bytecode, no JIT -- and no edit to the kernel, because Scala Native's javalib supplies the very java.lang.Thread and AtomicInteger the JVM rows use.
comments: One source, and the choice is a JVM or LLVM — no edit to the kernel, because Scala Native's javalib supplies the very java.lang.Thread and AtomicInteger the JVM rows use. The only Scala row whose ISA target is exact: clang takes the harness's -march verbatim, in both modes, where every JVM row can only approximate it — so its isa is what its mode asked for, and nothing else in this language group can say that. clang is handed -ffp-contract=off in every mode, for the reason c-clang is: it contracts into an FMA by default, and the checksum is not negotiable at any ISA target. Not to be confused with scala-native-image, which is GraalVM AOT-compiling JVM bytecode: a different compiler, a different runtime, and a confusingly similar name.

description: The same Mandelbrot.scala as scala-scalac-openjdk, byte for byte, compiled by Scala Native through LLVM into a standalone ELF. No JVM, no bytecode, no JIT -- and no edit to the kernel, because Scala Native's javalib supplies the very java.lang.Thread and AtomicInteger the JVM rows use.

Floating point is the JLS's, and Graal is bound by it exactly as C2 is.

description: The same Mandelbrot.scala as scala-scalac-openjdk, byte for byte, compiled by the same scalac and executed by Eclipse OpenJ9 instead of HotSpot.

description: The kernel in Scala 3, compiled by scalac to JVM bytecode and run on the same HotSpot as java-javac-openjdk and kotlin-kotlinc-openjdk. A plain while loop, deliberately: the algorithm is the contract, and a foldLeft over a lazy Iterator would measure the collections library instead of the backend.
comments: Floating point is the JVM's, inherited whole: a Double is an IEEE 754 double, and neither scalac nor the JIT may contract a multiply-add. One scalac invocation on one file — no sbt, no Gradle, no jar — so the build phase times the compiler and nothing else. Both the Scala 3 library and the 2.13 one it still stands on are on the classpath, and the JVM loads them at startup. The JVM is handed nothing else: no -Xmx, no cap on vector width, the defaults a Scala program gets.

description: The same mandelbrot.mts as ts-nodejs and ts-deno, byte for byte, executed by Bun on JavaScriptCore. The only TypeScript row not running on V8, which makes it the only one where a gap in compute time is about the engine.
comments: Bun strips the types on load and hands the JavaScript to JavaScriptCore: nothing type-checks this kernel here. The build phase is `bun build`, a transpile and a bundle — not the parse Node reports, and not the type check Deno reports.

description: The same mandelbrot.mts as ts-nodejs, byte for byte, executed by Deno -- the runtime that has run TypeScript natively since its first release, on the same V8 Node uses. If the head start shows up anywhere, it shows up here.
comments: The build phase is `deno check`, a real type check, where ts-nodejs runs a parse that erases the types without reading them. Only Deno's number says what type- checking this kernel costs; the other two never do it.

description: The kernel in TypeScript, executed by Node 24, which strips the types and runs the JavaScript underneath. Same worker_threads, same SharedArrayBuffer row queue, same arithmetic as js-nodejs -- the annotations are the only difference.
comments: Node erases the types syntactically and hands the JavaScript to V8 — no tsc, no emit, no type check. Every worker isolate loads the file and erases them again. The source is js-nodejs's, with annotations and nothing else added.

description: The reference kernel in Zig, compiled at -OReleaseFast against the ISA the mode names, with std.Thread and an atomic work queue. No libc: the binary is static, and its threads are raw clone syscalls rather than pthreads.
comments: Pinned to Zig 0.15.2. The 0.16 standard library rewrote the I/O and process APIs this kernel calls, and a benchmark that has to be rewritten for every release is measuring the release notes. -OReleaseFast does not relax the arithmetic: Zig's float mode is `.strict` unless the *source* says `@setFloatMode(.optimized)`, and this kernel does not say it. The binary is static and libc-free: it carries everything it needs, where the C rows load libc at run time.

description: The reference kernel in Zig, compiled at -OReleaseFast against the ISA the mode names, with std.Thread and an atomic work queue. No libc: the binary is static, and its threads are raw clone syscalls rather than pthreads.
comments: Pinned to Zig 0.15.2. The 0.16 standard library rewrote the I/O and process APIs this kernel calls, and a benchmark that has to be rewritten for every release is measuring the release notes. -OReleaseFast does not relax the arithmetic: Zig's float mode is `.strict` unless the *source* says `@setFloatMode(.optimized)`, and this kernel does not say it. The binary is static and libc-free: it carries everything it needs, where the C rows load libc at run time.

description: The same mandelbrot.c as c-gcc, byte for byte, compiled by clang at -O3 against the same ISA, on the same Debian, with the same flags. One source, two code generators: whatever separates these two rows is LLVM against GCC.
comments: Debian's clang, on the same base image, against the same libc and the same linker as c-gcc: the distribution and the standard library are held constant so that the code generator is the only thing that differs. Like gcc it links libc dynamically, so the binary it ships does not contain the C library.

description: The same mandelbrot.c as c-gcc, byte for byte, compiled by clang at -O3 against the same ISA, on the same Debian, with the same flags. One source, two code generators: whatever separates these two rows is LLVM against GCC.
comments: Debian's clang, on the same base image, against the same libc and the same linker as c-gcc: the distribution and the standard library are held constant so that the code generator is the only thing that differs. Like gcc it links libc dynamically, so the binary it ships does not contain the C library.

description: The reference kernel. Hand-written C, compiled by gcc at -O3 against the ISA the mode names, with std threads and an atomic work queue. Every other row in this table is read against this one.
comments: gcc links libc dynamically: the binary this row ships does not contain the C library. The code is in libc.so, outside the file the harness measures.

description: The reference kernel. Hand-written C, compiled by gcc at -O3 against the ISA the mode names, with std threads and an atomic work queue. Every other row in this table is read against this one.
comments: gcc links libc dynamically: the binary this row ships does not contain the C library. The code is in libc.so, outside the file the harness measures.

description: The same mandelbrot.cpp as cpp-gcc, byte for byte, compiled by clang++ at -O3 against the same ISA and -- this is the part that matters -- the same libstdc++. One source, one standard library, two code generators.
comments: Debian's clang++ links libstdc++, not libc++, and that is deliberate: swapping the standard library would be a second variable, and this row exists to isolate the first.

description: The same mandelbrot.cpp as cpp-gcc, byte for byte, compiled by clang++ at -O3 against the same ISA and -- this is the part that matters -- the same libstdc++. One source, one standard library, two code generators.
comments: Debian's clang++ links libstdc++, not libc++, and that is deliberate: swapping the standard library would be a second variable, and this row exists to isolate the first.

description: The C kernel written the way a C++ programmer would write it -- std::thread, std::atomic, std::vector, a lambda for the worker -- and compiled by the same GCC at -O3 on the same base image. The arithmetic is identical; the abstractions are not.
comments: `cpp` and not `c++`: the identity becomes an image tag, and a Docker tag admits no `+`.

description: The C kernel written the way a C++ programmer would write it -- std::thread, std::atomic, std::vector, a lambda for the worker -- and compiled by the same GCC at -O3 on the same base image. The arithmetic is identical; the abstractions are not.
comments: `cpp` and not `c++`: the identity becomes an image tag, and a Docker tag admits no `+`.

description: The reference kernel in Go, compiled by gc against the campaign's ISA target, with goroutines and an atomic row cursor. GOMAXPROCS is set from argv, never from the machine: Go 1.25 reads the cgroup quota by itself, and this benchmark refuses to ask that question.
comments: The float64() conversions in the source are rounding points, not casts, and they are load-bearing: written the natural way this kernel checksums 33209560 where every other backend produces 33209574, because gc fuses the multiply-adds and the arithmetic quietly stops being everyone else's. Strict floating point is therefore a property of the *source* here rather than of a flag — which is exactly why no mode of this backend can take it away, and why widening the ISA target cannot either. On that axis, `native` is the highest level the CPU is *known* to have, never the highest gc can name. The entrypoint reads the machine's own feature flags and picks the psABI level they clear, because naming the ceiling instead would be a claim: GOAMD64=v4 is AVX-512, the runtime verifies it, and on the AVX2 machines this campaign runs on that binary refuses to start — which would not be a row, it would be a quarantined unit and a table that never says what native buys Go. Detected, such a machine answers v3 — the baseline's own level, the same binary, and the two rows tie. On AArch64 the same rule gives v8.2, also the baseline's: gc accepts up to v9.5, nothing checks that level against the CPU, no machine that runs this is an ARMv9, and the hot loop disassembles byte for byte identically at v8.2 and v9.5. Where the two modes tie here, they tie because they are one program, and that is the result rather than a gap in it. gc also links the garbage collector and the scheduler into every binary — nothing is loaded from a shared runtime at startup.

description: The same Mandelbrot.java as java-javac-openjdk, byte for byte, compiled by the same javac and run on the same HotSpot -- with Graal replacing C2 as the JIT. Same bytecode, same VM, same GC: the code generator is the only variable.
comments: Floating point is strict here as everywhere on the JVM: the JLS binds the JIT as tightly as it binds javac, so Graal may no more contract a multiply-add than C2 may. The entrypoint passes -XX:+EnableJVMCI -XX:+UseJVMCICompiler explicitly rather than trusting a default: a silent fall back to C2 would make this row java-javac-openjdk under another name, publishing a difference that is really just noise. HotSpot refuses to start on a flag it does not know, so a future GraalVM that drops JVMCI fails the campaign instead of quietly measuring the wrong compiler.

description: The same Mandelbrot.java as java-javac-openjdk, byte for byte, compiled by the same javac and executed by Eclipse OpenJ9 instead of HotSpot. A different JIT and a different GC underneath identical bytecode: whatever separates the two rows is the virtual machine.
comments: Floating point is strict here too, and by the same rule: the JLS binds any JVM that claims the name, so Testarossa may no more contract a multiply-add than C2 may. One quirk is worth carrying, because it makes every future -XX: flag on this row suspect — OpenJ9 silently ignores options it does not know: -XX:CompleteNonsenseFlag=42 starts it happily, where HotSpot refuses to boot. A flag passed here can therefore pin nothing while looking as though it pinned something, and this entrypoint passes none: neither -Xquickstart nor -Xshareclasses, no vector cap, nothing. This is the VM as it ships.

description: The reference kernel in Java, compiled by javac and run on HotSpot with platform threads and an AtomicInteger work queue. No Maven, no Gradle, no jar: one file, one javac invocation, so the Build column times the compiler and nothing else.
comments: Floating point is strict here and could not be anything else: since JEP 306 the JLS forbids javac — and every JIT — from contracting a multiply-add, so fusing is Math.fma and the source has to write it. The JVM is handed nothing but the classpath: no -Xss, no -Xmx, no -XX:TieredStopAtLevel, no cap on vector width. The defaults are what a Java program gets. javac is what the Build column times; C2 compiles during the run, and its time is billed there.

description: The same Mandelbrot.java as java-javac-openjdk, byte for byte, compiled ahead of time by GraalVM native-image into a standalone ELF. No JVM at run time, no JIT, no class loading -- the program starts like a C binary because it is one.
comments: javac still runs, as a step in this compiler's pipeline rather than as the compiler: what ships is an ELF with the garbage collector and the runtime linked into it, and no JVM is installed in the image. The only JVM row with a true -march — though on AArch64 native-image offers no armv8.2-a and takes armv8.1-a: one level BELOW the campaign's baseline, never above, and the sample's isa column says armv8.1-a where its mode says baseline. Floating point is strict in both modes and cannot be otherwise: the JLS binds Graal ahead of time exactly as it binds C2 on the fly, so a wider ISA changes which instructions are emitted and never what they compute.

description: The same Mandelbrot.java as java-javac-openjdk, byte for byte, compiled ahead of time by GraalVM native-image into a standalone ELF. No JVM at run time, no JIT, no class loading -- the program starts like a C binary because it is one.
comments: javac still runs, as a step in this compiler's pipeline rather than as the compiler: what ships is an ELF with the garbage collector and the runtime linked into it, and no JVM is installed in the image. The only JVM row with a true -march — though on AArch64 native-image offers no armv8.2-a and takes armv8.1-a: one level BELOW the campaign's baseline, never above, and the sample's isa column says armv8.1-a where its mode says baseline. Floating point is strict in both modes and cannot be otherwise: the JLS binds Graal ahead of time exactly as it binds C2 on the fly, so a wider ISA changes which instructions are emitted and never what they compute.

description: The same mandelbrot.mjs as js-nodejs and js-deno, byte for byte, executed by Bun on JavaScriptCore. Node and Deno share V8; this is the only JavaScript row with a different engine underneath, which makes it the only one where a gap is about the compiler rather than the runtime around it.
comments: The build phase is `bun build` — a transpile and a bundle. Node's is a parse and Deno's a type check: the three runtimes do not agree on what happens before a run, and each build number describes its own runtime's idea of it.

description: The same mandelbrot.mjs as js-nodejs, byte for byte, executed by Deno -- which runs the same V8. Any gap between these two rows is the runtime around the engine: module resolution, the permission checks, and how quickly it can boot a worker isolate.
comments: The build phase is `deno check`, a full type check, where js-nodejs runs `node --check`, a parse. DENO_DIR ships warm with the node typings, because a measured run has no network to fetch them from.

description: The kernel as written, executed by Node.js on V8. Parallelism comes from node:worker_threads -- one isolate per worker -- with the row queue in a SharedArrayBuffer and Atomics.add handing rows out, which is as close to the C kernel's atomic cursor as the language gets.
comments: The build phase is `node --check` — parse and compile, no execution. The same mandelbrot.mjs runs under js-deno and js-bun, byte for byte, so what those three rows vary is the runtime and not the program.

description: The reference kernel in Julia, JIT-compiled through LLVM on first call, with Threads.@spawn tasks pulling rows off an atomic cursor. The ISA target is passed explicitly to the JIT, in both modes -- Julia is one of the few runtimes that compiles at run time and still lets you say which machine to compile for.
comments: The JIT compiles on first call, inside the measured run: a script's native code is cached nowhere, so every run pays for it again. There is no warm-up phase to hide that in, and hiding it would be a different benchmark. `--cpu-target` is passed on every invocation, and the baseline mode is the reason it must be: Julia's own default is `native`, so a forgotten flag does not fail the build -- it compiles for the bench machine and files the result under `baseline`. Floating-point needs no flag in either mode: Julia fuses a multiply-add only where the source writes `muladd`, and never reassociates.

description: The reference kernel in Julia, JIT-compiled through LLVM on first call, with Threads.@spawn tasks pulling rows off an atomic cursor. The ISA target is passed explicitly to the JIT, in both modes -- Julia is one of the few runtimes that compiles at run time and still lets you say which machine to compile for.

There is no warm-up phase to hide that in, and hiding it would be a different benchmark. `--cpu-target` is passed on every invocation, and the baseline mode is the reason it must be: Julia's own default is `native`, so a forgotten flag does not fail the build -- it compiles for the bench machine and files the result under `baseline`. Floating-point needs no flag in either mode: Julia fuses a multiply-add only where the source writes `muladd`, and never reassociates.

description: The same Mandelbrot.kt as kotlin-kotlinc-openjdk, byte for byte, compiled by the same kotlinc -- with Graal replacing C2 as the JIT. One cell of the (language x JVM) grid.
comments: One cell of the (language × JVM) grid: same bytecode, same VM, same GC, a different code generator. The entrypoint passes -XX:+EnableJVMCI -XX:+UseJVMCICompiler explicitly rather than trusting a default: a silent fall back to C2 would make this row kotlin-kotlinc-openjdk under another name. Floating point is the JLS's, and Graal is bound by it exactly as C2 is.

description: The same Mandelbrot.kt as kotlin-kotlinc-openjdk, byte for byte, compiled by the same kotlinc and executed by Eclipse OpenJ9 instead of HotSpot.
comments: A different virtual machine, not a different build of HotSpot: OpenJ9 brings its own JIT (Testarossa), its own GC and its own object layout, under bytecode that is byte for byte the other Kotlin rows'. Floating point is still the JLS's, and still strict. One quirk is worth carrying, because it makes any future -XX: flag on this row suspect: OpenJ9 silently ignores options it does not know, where HotSpot refuses to boot on them. This entrypoint passes none.

description: The kernel in Kotlin, compiled by kotlinc to JVM bytecode and run on the same HotSpot as java-javac-openjdk. Same platform threads, same AtomicInteger queue, same arithmetic -- the language in front of the JIT is the only thing that changes.
comments: Floating point is the JVM's, inherited whole: a Double is an IEEE 754 double, and neither kotlinc nor the JIT may contract a multiply-add. One kotlinc invocation on one file — no Gradle, no Maven, no jar — so the build phase times the compiler and nothing else. The Kotlin standard library is on the classpath, and the JVM loads it at startup. The JVM is handed nothing else: no -Xmx, no cap on vector width, the defaults a Kotlin program gets.

description: The same Mandelbrot.kt as every other Kotlin row, compiled ahead of time by GraalVM native-image into a standalone ELF -- with the Kotlin standard library swallowed whole and linked in. kotlinc runs first, as a step in this compiler's pipeline, exactly as javac does for java-native-image.
comments: kotlinc runs first, as a step in this compiler's pipeline, exactly as javac does for java-native-image; native-image then has to analyse and link the Kotlin standard library into the ELF. On AArch64 it takes armv8.1-a: one level BELOW the campaign's baseline, never above, and the sample's isa column says so where its mode says baseline. Floating point is strict in both modes and cannot be otherwise: the JLS binds Graal ahead of time exactly as it binds C2 on the fly, so a wider ISA changes which instructions are emitted and never what they compute.

description: The same Mandelbrot.kt as every other Kotlin row, compiled ahead of time by GraalVM native-image into a standalone ELF -- with the Kotlin standard library swallowed whole and linked in. kotlinc runs first, as a step in this compiler's pipeline, exactly as javac does for java-native-image.
comments: kotlinc runs first, as a step in this compiler's pipeline, exactly as javac does for java-native-image; native-image then has to analyse and link the Kotlin standard library into the ELF. On AArch64 it takes armv8.1-a: one level BELOW the campaign's baseline, never above, and the sample's isa column says so where its mode says baseline. Floating point is strict in both modes and cannot be otherwise: the JLS binds Graal ahead of time exactly as it binds C2 on the fly, so a wider ISA changes which instructions are emitted and never what they compute.

description: The kernel as written, executed by the CPython interpreter. Parallelism comes from multiprocessing with the fork start method, because the GIL serialises CPU-bound bytecode -- threading here would measure the GIL, not the machine.
comments: Forking the pool is inside the timer on purpose: what a parallel runtime costs to start is part of what that runtime costs. The row reports `isa: distro`, not the campaign's baseline: no `-march` appears in this interpreter's own CFLAGS or configure line, so its eval loop was built for the toolchain's floor -- plain `x86-64` (v1) -- one level below the baseline every compiled row here was held to, and chosen by the packager rather than by this campaign. What that costs is bounded by what the code is: an eval loop is a branchy dispatch over one bytecode at a time, with nothing to vectorise, so the instructions v3 adds over v1 are mostly instructions it has no use for. It goes in a column because it is true, not because it is large.

description: The same mandelbrot.py as python-cpython, byte for byte, compiled by Cython to a C extension module instead of interpreted. Cython is the compiler here, exactly as gcc is for the C kernel -- not a library the kernel imports.
comments: The source is python-cpython's, byte for byte, and that includes the absence of type annotations — annotating it would be a different program, and this row is here to compile the same one. Unannotated, the generated C manipulates PyFloat objects through the C-API rather than raw doubles. gcc is handed `-ffp-contract=off` in every mode: the ISA target decides which instructions it may emit, never what the arithmetic means. The `isa` it reports is the `-march` it was given, verbatim — gcc is the vocabulary the harness already speaks.

description: The same mandelbrot.py as python-cpython, byte for byte, compiled by Cython to a C extension module instead of interpreted. Cython is the compiler here, exactly as gcc is for the C kernel -- not a library the kernel imports.
comments: The source is python-cpython's, byte for byte, and that includes the absence of type annotations — annotating it would be a different program, and this row is here to compile the same one. Unannotated, the generated C manipulates PyFloat objects through the C-API rather than raw doubles. gcc is handed `-ffp-contract=off` in every mode: the ISA target decides which instructions it may emit, never what the arithmetic means. The `isa` it reports is the `-march` it was given, verbatim — gcc is the vocabulary the harness already speaks.

description: The same mandelbrot.py as python-cpython, byte for byte, executed by PyPy. The source is unchanged, the multiprocessing pool is unchanged; the difference is a tracing JIT that turns the hot loop into machine code while it runs.
comments: The JIT compiles the hot loop inside the measured run, because that is where it happens: there is no warm-up phase to hide it in, and hiding it would be a different benchmark. The source and the multiprocessing pool are python-cpython's, unchanged.

description: The reference kernel in Rust, compiled by rustc at -O3 against the ISA the mode names, with std::thread scoped threads and an AtomicU32 work queue. No cargo, no rayon, no dependency: one file, one rustc invocation.
comments: rustc only *warns* when it does not recognise an ISA target, then hands back a generic binary — so the entrypoint translates the harness's gcc-spelled -march into LLVM's spelling, and reads rustc's stderr back to refuse a binary it ignored the flag for. It links std statically: the binary carries the Rust runtime rather than loading it.

description: The reference kernel in Rust, compiled by rustc at -O3 against the ISA the mode names, with std::thread scoped threads and an AtomicU32 work queue. No cargo, no rayon, no dependency: one file, one rustc invocation.
comments: rustc only *warns* when it does not recognise an ISA target, then hands back a generic binary — so the entrypoint translates the harness's gcc-spelled -march into LLVM's spelling, and reads rustc's stderr back to refuse a binary it ignored the flag for. It links std statically: the binary carries the Rust runtime rather than loading it.

description: The same Mandelbrot.scala as every other Scala row, compiled ahead of time by GraalVM native-image into a standalone ELF -- with the Scala runtime linked in. scalac runs first, as a step in this compiler's pipeline.
comments: scalac runs first, as a step in this compiler's pipeline; native-image then analyses and links the whole Scala runtime — the 3.x library plus the 2.13 one it still stands on — into the ELF. On AArch64 it takes armv8.1-a: one level BELOW the campaign's baseline, never above, and the sample's isa column says so where its mode says baseline. Floating point is strict in both modes and cannot be otherwise: the JLS binds Graal ahead of time exactly as it binds C2 on the fly. Not to be confused with scala-scala-native, which is Scala Native through LLVM: a different compiler entirely, and one that takes the -march verbatim.

description: The same Mandelbrot.scala as every other Scala row, compiled ahead of time by GraalVM native-image into a standalone ELF -- with the Scala runtime linked in. scalac runs first, as a step in this compiler's pipeline.
comments: scalac runs first, as a step in this compiler's pipeline; native-image then analyses and links the whole Scala runtime — the 3.x library plus the 2.13 one it still stands on — into the ELF. On AArch64 it takes armv8.1-a: one level BELOW the campaign's baseline, never above, and the sample's isa column says so where its mode says baseline. Floating point is strict in both modes and cannot be otherwise: the JLS binds Graal ahead of time exactly as it binds C2 on the fly. Not to be confused with scala-scala-native, which is Scala Native through LLVM: a different compiler entirely, and one that takes the -march verbatim.

description: The same Mandelbrot.scala as scala-scalac-openjdk, byte for byte, compiled by Scala Native through LLVM into a standalone ELF. No JVM, no bytecode, no JIT -- and no edit to the kernel, because Scala Native's javalib supplies the very java.lang.Thread and AtomicInteger the JVM rows use.
comments: One source, and the choice is a JVM or LLVM — no edit to the kernel, because Scala Native's javalib supplies the very java.lang.Thread and AtomicInteger the JVM rows use. The only Scala row whose ISA target is exact: clang takes the harness's -march verbatim, in both modes, where every JVM row can only approximate it — so its isa is what its mode asked for, and nothing else in this language group can say that. clang is handed -ffp-contract=off in every mode, for the reason c-clang is: it contracts into an FMA by default, and the checksum is not negotiable at any ISA target. Not to be confused with scala-native-image, which is GraalVM AOT-compiling JVM bytecode: a different compiler, a different runtime, and a confusingly similar name.

description: The same Mandelbrot.scala as scala-scalac-openjdk, byte for byte, compiled by Scala Native through LLVM into a standalone ELF. No JVM, no bytecode, no JIT -- and no edit to the kernel, because Scala Native's javalib supplies the very java.lang.Thread and AtomicInteger the JVM rows use.

Floating point is the JLS's, and Graal is bound by it exactly as C2 is.

description: The same Mandelbrot.scala as scala-scalac-openjdk, byte for byte, compiled by the same scalac and executed by Eclipse OpenJ9 instead of HotSpot.

description: The kernel in Scala 3, compiled by scalac to JVM bytecode and run on the same HotSpot as java-javac-openjdk and kotlin-kotlinc-openjdk. A plain while loop, deliberately: the algorithm is the contract, and a foldLeft over a lazy Iterator would measure the collections library instead of the backend.
comments: Floating point is the JVM's, inherited whole: a Double is an IEEE 754 double, and neither scalac nor the JIT may contract a multiply-add. One scalac invocation on one file — no sbt, no Gradle, no jar — so the build phase times the compiler and nothing else. Both the Scala 3 library and the 2.13 one it still stands on are on the classpath, and the JVM loads them at startup. The JVM is handed nothing else: no -Xmx, no cap on vector width, the defaults a Scala program gets.

description: The same mandelbrot.mts as ts-nodejs and ts-deno, byte for byte, executed by Bun on JavaScriptCore. The only TypeScript row not running on V8, which makes it the only one where a gap in compute time is about the engine.
comments: Bun strips the types on load and hands the JavaScript to JavaScriptCore: nothing type-checks this kernel here. The build phase is `bun build`, a transpile and a bundle — not the parse Node reports, and not the type check Deno reports.

description: The same mandelbrot.mts as ts-nodejs, byte for byte, executed by Deno -- the runtime that has run TypeScript natively since its first release, on the same V8 Node uses. If the head start shows up anywhere, it shows up here.
comments: The build phase is `deno check`, a real type check, where ts-nodejs runs a parse that erases the types without reading them. Only Deno's number says what type- checking this kernel costs; the other two never do it.

description: The kernel in TypeScript, executed by Node 24, which strips the types and runs the JavaScript underneath. Same worker_threads, same SharedArrayBuffer row queue, same arithmetic as js-nodejs -- the annotations are the only difference.
comments: Node erases the types syntactically and hands the JavaScript to V8 — no tsc, no emit, no type check. Every worker isolate loads the file and erases them again. The source is js-nodejs's, with annotations and nothing else added.

description: The reference kernel in Zig, compiled at -OReleaseFast against the ISA the mode names, with std.Thread and an atomic work queue. No libc: the binary is static, and its threads are raw clone syscalls rather than pthreads.
comments: Pinned to Zig 0.15.2. The 0.16 standard library rewrote the I/O and process APIs this kernel calls, and a benchmark that has to be rewritten for every release is measuring the release notes. -OReleaseFast does not relax the arithmetic: Zig's float mode is `.strict` unless the *source* says `@setFloatMode(.optimized)`, and this kernel does not say it. The binary is static and libc-free: it carries everything it needs, where the C rows load libc at run time.

description: The reference kernel in Zig, compiled at -OReleaseFast against the ISA the mode names, with std.Thread and an atomic work queue. No libc: the binary is static, and its threads are raw clone syscalls rather than pthreads.
comments: Pinned to Zig 0.15.2. The 0.16 standard library rewrote the I/O and process APIs this kernel calls, and a benchmark that has to be rewritten for every release is measuring the release notes. -OReleaseFast does not relax the arithmetic: Zig's float mode is `.strict` unless the *source* says `@setFloatMode(.optimized)`, and this kernel does not say it. The binary is static and libc-free: it carries everything it needs, where the C rows load libc at run time.

description: The same mandelbrot.c as c-gcc, byte for byte, compiled by clang at -O3 against the same ISA, on the same Debian, with the same flags. One source, two code generators: whatever separates these two rows is LLVM against GCC.
comments: Debian's clang, on the same base image, against the same libc and the same linker as c-gcc: the distribution and the standard library are held constant so that the code generator is the only thing that differs. Like gcc it links libc dynamically, so the binary it ships does not contain the C library.

description: The same mandelbrot.c as c-gcc, byte for byte, compiled by clang at -O3 against the same ISA, on the same Debian, with the same flags. One source, two code generators: whatever separates these two rows is LLVM against GCC.
comments: Debian's clang, on the same base image, against the same libc and the same linker as c-gcc: the distribution and the standard library are held constant so that the code generator is the only thing that differs. Like gcc it links libc dynamically, so the binary it ships does not contain the C library.

description: The reference kernel. Hand-written C, compiled by gcc at -O3 against the ISA the mode names, with std threads and an atomic work queue. Every other row in this table is read against this one.
comments: gcc links libc dynamically: the binary this row ships does not contain the C library. The code is in libc.so, outside the file the harness measures.

description: The reference kernel. Hand-written C, compiled by gcc at -O3 against the ISA the mode names, with std threads and an atomic work queue. Every other row in this table is read against this one.
comments: gcc links libc dynamically: the binary this row ships does not contain the C library. The code is in libc.so, outside the file the harness measures.

description: The same mandelbrot.cpp as cpp-gcc, byte for byte, compiled by clang++ at -O3 against the same ISA and -- this is the part that matters -- the same libstdc++. One source, one standard library, two code generators.
comments: Debian's clang++ links libstdc++, not libc++, and that is deliberate: swapping the standard library would be a second variable, and this row exists to isolate the first.

description: The same mandelbrot.cpp as cpp-gcc, byte for byte, compiled by clang++ at -O3 against the same ISA and -- this is the part that matters -- the same libstdc++. One source, one standard library, two code generators.
comments: Debian's clang++ links libstdc++, not libc++, and that is deliberate: swapping the standard library would be a second variable, and this row exists to isolate the first.

description: The C kernel written the way a C++ programmer would write it -- std::thread, std::atomic, std::vector, a lambda for the worker -- and compiled by the same GCC at -O3 on the same base image. The arithmetic is identical; the abstractions are not.
comments: `cpp` and not `c++`: the identity becomes an image tag, and a Docker tag admits no `+`.

description: The C kernel written the way a C++ programmer would write it -- std::thread, std::atomic, std::vector, a lambda for the worker -- and compiled by the same GCC at -O3 on the same base image. The arithmetic is identical; the abstractions are not.
comments: `cpp` and not `c++`: the identity becomes an image tag, and a Docker tag admits no `+`.

description: The reference kernel in Go, compiled by gc against the campaign's ISA target, with goroutines and an atomic row cursor. GOMAXPROCS is set from argv, never from the machine: Go 1.25 reads the cgroup quota by itself, and this benchmark refuses to ask that question.
comments: The float64() conversions in the source are rounding points, not casts, and they are load-bearing: written the natural way this kernel checksums 33209560 where every other backend produces 33209574, because gc fuses the multiply-adds and the arithmetic quietly stops being everyone else's. Strict floating point is therefore a property of the *source* here rather than of a flag — which is exactly why no mode of this backend can take it away, and why widening the ISA target cannot either. On that axis, `native` is the highest level the CPU is *known* to have, never the highest gc can name. The entrypoint reads the machine's own feature flags and picks the psABI level they clear, because naming the ceiling instead would be a claim: GOAMD64=v4 is AVX-512, the runtime verifies it, and on the AVX2 machines this campaign runs on that binary refuses to start — which would not be a row, it would be a quarantined unit and a table that never says what native buys Go. Detected, such a machine answers v3 — the baseline's own level, the same binary, and the two rows tie. On AArch64 the same rule gives v8.2, also the baseline's: gc accepts up to v9.5, nothing checks that level against the CPU, no machine that runs this is an ARMv9, and the hot loop disassembles byte for byte identically at v8.2 and v9.5. Where the two modes tie here, they tie because they are one program, and that is the result rather than a gap in it. gc also links the garbage collector and the scheduler into every binary — nothing is loaded from a shared runtime at startup.

description: The same Mandelbrot.java as java-javac-openjdk, byte for byte, compiled by the same javac and run on the same HotSpot -- with Graal replacing C2 as the JIT. Same bytecode, same VM, same GC: the code generator is the only variable.
comments: Floating point is strict here as everywhere on the JVM: the JLS binds the JIT as tightly as it binds javac, so Graal may no more contract a multiply-add than C2 may. The entrypoint passes -XX:+EnableJVMCI -XX:+UseJVMCICompiler explicitly rather than trusting a default: a silent fall back to C2 would make this row java-javac-openjdk under another name, publishing a difference that is really just noise. HotSpot refuses to start on a flag it does not know, so a future GraalVM that drops JVMCI fails the campaign instead of quietly measuring the wrong compiler.

description: The same Mandelbrot.java as java-javac-openjdk, byte for byte, compiled by the same javac and executed by Eclipse OpenJ9 instead of HotSpot. A different JIT and a different GC underneath identical bytecode: whatever separates the two rows is the virtual machine.
comments: Floating point is strict here too, and by the same rule: the JLS binds any JVM that claims the name, so Testarossa may no more contract a multiply-add than C2 may. One quirk is worth carrying, because it makes every future -XX: flag on this row suspect — OpenJ9 silently ignores options it does not know: -XX:CompleteNonsenseFlag=42 starts it happily, where HotSpot refuses to boot. A flag passed here can therefore pin nothing while looking as though it pinned something, and this entrypoint passes none: neither -Xquickstart nor -Xshareclasses, no vector cap, nothing. This is the VM as it ships.

description: The reference kernel in Java, compiled by javac and run on HotSpot with platform threads and an AtomicInteger work queue. No Maven, no Gradle, no jar: one file, one javac invocation, so the Build column times the compiler and nothing else.
comments: Floating point is strict here and could not be anything else: since JEP 306 the JLS forbids javac — and every JIT — from contracting a multiply-add, so fusing is Math.fma and the source has to write it. The JVM is handed nothing but the classpath: no -Xss, no -Xmx, no -XX:TieredStopAtLevel, no cap on vector width. The defaults are what a Java program gets. javac is what the Build column times; C2 compiles during the run, and its time is billed there.

description: The same Mandelbrot.java as java-javac-openjdk, byte for byte, compiled ahead of time by GraalVM native-image into a standalone ELF. No JVM at run time, no JIT, no class loading -- the program starts like a C binary because it is one.
comments: javac still runs, as a step in this compiler's pipeline rather than as the compiler: what ships is an ELF with the garbage collector and the runtime linked into it, and no JVM is installed in the image. The only JVM row with a true -march — though on AArch64 native-image offers no armv8.2-a and takes armv8.1-a: one level BELOW the campaign's baseline, never above, and the sample's isa column says armv8.1-a where its mode says baseline. Floating point is strict in both modes and cannot be otherwise: the JLS binds Graal ahead of time exactly as it binds C2 on the fly, so a wider ISA changes which instructions are emitted and never what they compute.

description: The same Mandelbrot.java as java-javac-openjdk, byte for byte, compiled ahead of time by GraalVM native-image into a standalone ELF. No JVM at run time, no JIT, no class loading -- the program starts like a C binary because it is one.
comments: javac still runs, as a step in this compiler's pipeline rather than as the compiler: what ships is an ELF with the garbage collector and the runtime linked into it, and no JVM is installed in the image. The only JVM row with a true -march — though on AArch64 native-image offers no armv8.2-a and takes armv8.1-a: one level BELOW the campaign's baseline, never above, and the sample's isa column says armv8.1-a where its mode says baseline. Floating point is strict in both modes and cannot be otherwise: the JLS binds Graal ahead of time exactly as it binds C2 on the fly, so a wider ISA changes which instructions are emitted and never what they compute.

description: The same mandelbrot.mjs as js-nodejs and js-deno, byte for byte, executed by Bun on JavaScriptCore. Node and Deno share V8; this is the only JavaScript row with a different engine underneath, which makes it the only one where a gap is about the compiler rather than the runtime around it.
comments: The build phase is `bun build` — a transpile and a bundle. Node's is a parse and Deno's a type check: the three runtimes do not agree on what happens before a run, and each build number describes its own runtime's idea of it.

description: The same mandelbrot.mjs as js-nodejs, byte for byte, executed by Deno -- which runs the same V8. Any gap between these two rows is the runtime around the engine: module resolution, the permission checks, and how quickly it can boot a worker isolate.
comments: The build phase is `deno check`, a full type check, where js-nodejs runs `node --check`, a parse. DENO_DIR ships warm with the node typings, because a measured run has no network to fetch them from.

description: The kernel as written, executed by Node.js on V8. Parallelism comes from node:worker_threads -- one isolate per worker -- with the row queue in a SharedArrayBuffer and Atomics.add handing rows out, which is as close to the C kernel's atomic cursor as the language gets.
comments: The build phase is `node --check` — parse and compile, no execution. The same mandelbrot.mjs runs under js-deno and js-bun, byte for byte, so what those three rows vary is the runtime and not the program.

description: The reference kernel in Julia, JIT-compiled through LLVM on first call, with Threads.@spawn tasks pulling rows off an atomic cursor. The ISA target is passed explicitly to the JIT, in both modes -- Julia is one of the few runtimes that compiles at run time and still lets you say which machine to compile for.
comments: The JIT compiles on first call, inside the measured run: a script's native code is cached nowhere, so every run pays for it again. There is no warm-up phase to hide that in, and hiding it would be a different benchmark. `--cpu-target` is passed on every invocation, and the baseline mode is the reason it must be: Julia's own default is `native`, so a forgotten flag does not fail the build -- it compiles for the bench machine and files the result under `baseline`. Floating-point needs no flag in either mode: Julia fuses a multiply-add only where the source writes `muladd`, and never reassociates.

description: The reference kernel in Julia, JIT-compiled through LLVM on first call, with Threads.@spawn tasks pulling rows off an atomic cursor. The ISA target is passed explicitly to the JIT, in both modes -- Julia is one of the few runtimes that compiles at run time and still lets you say which machine to compile for.

There is no warm-up phase to hide that in, and hiding it would be a different benchmark. `--cpu-target` is passed on every invocation, and the baseline mode is the reason it must be: Julia's own default is `native`, so a forgotten flag does not fail the build -- it compiles for the bench machine and files the result under `baseline`. Floating-point needs no flag in either mode: Julia fuses a multiply-add only where the source writes `muladd`, and never reassociates.

description: The same Mandelbrot.kt as kotlin-kotlinc-openjdk, byte for byte, compiled by the same kotlinc -- with Graal replacing C2 as the JIT. One cell of the (language x JVM) grid.
comments: One cell of the (language × JVM) grid: same bytecode, same VM, same GC, a different code generator. The entrypoint passes -XX:+EnableJVMCI -XX:+UseJVMCICompiler explicitly rather than trusting a default: a silent fall back to C2 would make this row kotlin-kotlinc-openjdk under another name. Floating point is the JLS's, and Graal is bound by it exactly as C2 is.

description: The same Mandelbrot.kt as kotlin-kotlinc-openjdk, byte for byte, compiled by the same kotlinc and executed by Eclipse OpenJ9 instead of HotSpot.
comments: A different virtual machine, not a different build of HotSpot: OpenJ9 brings its own JIT (Testarossa), its own GC and its own object layout, under bytecode that is byte for byte the other Kotlin rows'. Floating point is still the JLS's, and still strict. One quirk is worth carrying, because it makes any future -XX: flag on this row suspect: OpenJ9 silently ignores options it does not know, where HotSpot refuses to boot on them. This entrypoint passes none.

description: The kernel in Kotlin, compiled by kotlinc to JVM bytecode and run on the same HotSpot as java-javac-openjdk. Same platform threads, same AtomicInteger queue, same arithmetic -- the language in front of the JIT is the only thing that changes.
comments: Floating point is the JVM's, inherited whole: a Double is an IEEE 754 double, and neither kotlinc nor the JIT may contract a multiply-add. One kotlinc invocation on one file — no Gradle, no Maven, no jar — so the build phase times the compiler and nothing else. The Kotlin standard library is on the classpath, and the JVM loads it at startup. The JVM is handed nothing else: no -Xmx, no cap on vector width, the defaults a Kotlin program gets.

description: The same Mandelbrot.kt as every other Kotlin row, compiled ahead of time by GraalVM native-image into a standalone ELF -- with the Kotlin standard library swallowed whole and linked in. kotlinc runs first, as a step in this compiler's pipeline, exactly as javac does for java-native-image.
comments: kotlinc runs first, as a step in this compiler's pipeline, exactly as javac does for java-native-image; native-image then has to analyse and link the Kotlin standard library into the ELF. On AArch64 it takes armv8.1-a: one level BELOW the campaign's baseline, never above, and the sample's isa column says so where its mode says baseline. Floating point is strict in both modes and cannot be otherwise: the JLS binds Graal ahead of time exactly as it binds C2 on the fly, so a wider ISA changes which instructions are emitted and never what they compute.

description: The same Mandelbrot.kt as every other Kotlin row, compiled ahead of time by GraalVM native-image into a standalone ELF -- with the Kotlin standard library swallowed whole and linked in. kotlinc runs first, as a step in this compiler's pipeline, exactly as javac does for java-native-image.
comments: kotlinc runs first, as a step in this compiler's pipeline, exactly as javac does for java-native-image; native-image then has to analyse and link the Kotlin standard library into the ELF. On AArch64 it takes armv8.1-a: one level BELOW the campaign's baseline, never above, and the sample's isa column says so where its mode says baseline. Floating point is strict in both modes and cannot be otherwise: the JLS binds Graal ahead of time exactly as it binds C2 on the fly, so a wider ISA changes which instructions are emitted and never what they compute.

description: The kernel as written, executed by the CPython interpreter. Parallelism comes from multiprocessing with the fork start method, because the GIL serialises CPU-bound bytecode -- threading here would measure the GIL, not the machine.
comments: Forking the pool is inside the timer on purpose: what a parallel runtime costs to start is part of what that runtime costs. The row reports `isa: distro`, not the campaign's baseline: no `-march` appears in this interpreter's own CFLAGS or configure line, so its eval loop was built for the toolchain's floor -- plain `x86-64` (v1) -- one level below the baseline every compiled row here was held to, and chosen by the packager rather than by this campaign. What that costs is bounded by what the code is: an eval loop is a branchy dispatch over one bytecode at a time, with nothing to vectorise, so the instructions v3 adds over v1 are mostly instructions it has no use for. It goes in a column because it is true, not because it is large.

description: The same mandelbrot.py as python-cpython, byte for byte, compiled by Cython to a C extension module instead of interpreted. Cython is the compiler here, exactly as gcc is for the C kernel -- not a library the kernel imports.
comments: The source is python-cpython's, byte for byte, and that includes the absence of type annotations — annotating it would be a different program, and this row is here to compile the same one. Unannotated, the generated C manipulates PyFloat objects through the C-API rather than raw doubles. gcc is handed `-ffp-contract=off` in every mode: the ISA target decides which instructions it may emit, never what the arithmetic means. The `isa` it reports is the `-march` it was given, verbatim — gcc is the vocabulary the harness already speaks.

description: The same mandelbrot.py as python-cpython, byte for byte, compiled by Cython to a C extension module instead of interpreted. Cython is the compiler here, exactly as gcc is for the C kernel -- not a library the kernel imports.
comments: The source is python-cpython's, byte for byte, and that includes the absence of type annotations — annotating it would be a different program, and this row is here to compile the same one. Unannotated, the generated C manipulates PyFloat objects through the C-API rather than raw doubles. gcc is handed `-ffp-contract=off` in every mode: the ISA target decides which instructions it may emit, never what the arithmetic means. The `isa` it reports is the `-march` it was given, verbatim — gcc is the vocabulary the harness already speaks.

description: The same mandelbrot.py as python-cpython, byte for byte, executed by PyPy. The source is unchanged, the multiprocessing pool is unchanged; the difference is a tracing JIT that turns the hot loop into machine code while it runs.
comments: The JIT compiles the hot loop inside the measured run, because that is where it happens: there is no warm-up phase to hide it in, and hiding it would be a different benchmark. The source and the multiprocessing pool are python-cpython's, unchanged.

description: The reference kernel in Rust, compiled by rustc at -O3 against the ISA the mode names, with std::thread scoped threads and an AtomicU32 work queue. No cargo, no rayon, no dependency: one file, one rustc invocation.
comments: rustc only *warns* when it does not recognise an ISA target, then hands back a generic binary — so the entrypoint translates the harness's gcc-spelled -march into LLVM's spelling, and reads rustc's stderr back to refuse a binary it ignored the flag for. It links std statically: the binary carries the Rust runtime rather than loading it.

description: The reference kernel in Rust, compiled by rustc at -O3 against the ISA the mode names, with std::thread scoped threads and an AtomicU32 work queue. No cargo, no rayon, no dependency: one file, one rustc invocation.
comments: rustc only *warns* when it does not recognise an ISA target, then hands back a generic binary — so the entrypoint translates the harness's gcc-spelled -march into LLVM's spelling, and reads rustc's stderr back to refuse a binary it ignored the flag for. It links std statically: the binary carries the Rust runtime rather than loading it.

description: The same Mandelbrot.scala as every other Scala row, compiled ahead of time by GraalVM native-image into a standalone ELF -- with the Scala runtime linked in. scalac runs first, as a step in this compiler's pipeline.
comments: scalac runs first, as a step in this compiler's pipeline; native-image then analyses and links the whole Scala runtime — the 3.x library plus the 2.13 one it still stands on — into the ELF. On AArch64 it takes armv8.1-a: one level BELOW the campaign's baseline, never above, and the sample's isa column says so where its mode says baseline. Floating point is strict in both modes and cannot be otherwise: the JLS binds Graal ahead of time exactly as it binds C2 on the fly. Not to be confused with scala-scala-native, which is Scala Native through LLVM: a different compiler entirely, and one that takes the -march verbatim.

description: The same Mandelbrot.scala as every other Scala row, compiled ahead of time by GraalVM native-image into a standalone ELF -- with the Scala runtime linked in. scalac runs first, as a step in this compiler's pipeline.
comments: scalac runs first, as a step in this compiler's pipeline; native-image then analyses and links the whole Scala runtime — the 3.x library plus the 2.13 one it still stands on — into the ELF. On AArch64 it takes armv8.1-a: one level BELOW the campaign's baseline, never above, and the sample's isa column says so where its mode says baseline. Floating point is strict in both modes and cannot be otherwise: the JLS binds Graal ahead of time exactly as it binds C2 on the fly. Not to be confused with scala-scala-native, which is Scala Native through LLVM: a different compiler entirely, and one that takes the -march verbatim.

description: The same Mandelbrot.scala as scala-scalac-openjdk, byte for byte, compiled by Scala Native through LLVM into a standalone ELF. No JVM, no bytecode, no JIT -- and no edit to the kernel, because Scala Native's javalib supplies the very java.lang.Thread and AtomicInteger the JVM rows use.
comments: One source, and the choice is a JVM or LLVM — no edit to the kernel, because Scala Native's javalib supplies the very java.lang.Thread and AtomicInteger the JVM rows use. The only Scala row whose ISA target is exact: clang takes the harness's -march verbatim, in both modes, where every JVM row can only approximate it — so its isa is what its mode asked for, and nothing else in this language group can say that. clang is handed -ffp-contract=off in every mode, for the reason c-clang is: it contracts into an FMA by default, and the checksum is not negotiable at any ISA target. Not to be confused with scala-native-image, which is GraalVM AOT-compiling JVM bytecode: a different compiler, a different runtime, and a confusingly similar name.

description: The same Mandelbrot.scala as scala-scalac-openjdk, byte for byte, compiled by Scala Native through LLVM into a standalone ELF. No JVM, no bytecode, no JIT -- and no edit to the kernel, because Scala Native's javalib supplies the very java.lang.Thread and AtomicInteger the JVM rows use.

Floating point is the JLS's, and Graal is bound by it exactly as C2 is.

description: The same Mandelbrot.scala as scala-scalac-openjdk, byte for byte, compiled by the same scalac and executed by Eclipse OpenJ9 instead of HotSpot.
